title: Using Hacer and Verb Conjugations
description: Learn to use the verb 'hacer' and understand Spanish verb conjugations for different subjects.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: To do or to make in Spanish is hacer. It has a silent h, spelled h-a-c-e-r. In most of the Spanish-speaking world, it's pronounced ha/s/er. What is the verb ending?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: -er verb. Whenever we come across a new verb, note the ending. If we know it's an -er verb, we know how to work with it. How would you say 'he's doing' or 'she's making'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hace. Good. How would you say 'you're doing it'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo hace. How would you say 'they're doing it' or 'you guys are doing it'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo hacen. For informal 'you', we add an 's'. How do you say 'you're doing it' informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo haces. Good. The word for 'what' in Spanish is 'qué'. How do you say 'what are you doing'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿Qué haces? This can also mean 'how are you' or 'what are you up to'. For -ar verbs, we add 'n' for 'they' or 'you (plural)', and 's' for informal 'you'. For -er/-ir verbs, we add 's' for informal 'you', 'n' for 'they', 'you (plural)'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Puedo in Spanish means 'I can' or 'I am able'. How would you say 'he can' or 'she can'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Puede. And informally, 'you can'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Puedes. How would you say 'can you come?' informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿Puedes venir? How about 'can't you come?'

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿No puedes venir? Why can't you come?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿Por qué no puedes venir? I want to know why you can't come.

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: quiero saber por qué no puedes venir

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Quiero saber por qué no puedes venir. Well done. Let's recap: for -ar verbs, we remove the ending for 'I' and add 'o'. For -er/-ir verbs, we do the same. Vender is 'to sell', and 'I sell' is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Vendo. Comprar is 'to buy', and 'I buy' is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Compro. Llegar is 'to arrive', and 'I arrive' is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Llego. Some verbs are irregular, like salir, which means 'to leave'. If we apply the rule, we might say 'salo', but it's actually 'salgo'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esperar means 'to wait' or 'to hope'. How do you say 'I wait' or 'I hope'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Espero. How do you say 'he waits'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Espera. How do you say 'they wait'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esperan. If you want to say 'they're waiting for me', you say 'me esperan'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Me esperan. How do you say 'they're not waiting for me'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No me esperan. I want to know why they're not waiting for me.

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: quiero saber por qué no me esperan

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Quiero saber por qué no me esperan. Well done. Words ending in -ary become -ario in Spanish. For example, 'necessary' becomes 'necesario'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'contrary'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Contrario. Good.

outro.text: You've learned how to use the verb 'hacer' and understand verb conjugations for different subjects.
Try using 'hacer' in different sentences. How would you say 'we are making it' or 'they are doing it'?
Practice these conjugations with other verbs you know. The more you use them, the more natural they'll become.
